definitions.text: Used to express the future tense, sometimes with an implication of volition or determination when used in the first person. Compare shall. .mw-parser-output .defdate{font-size:smaller}
examples: Will you be doing the shopping this evening? If so, will you do mine too, please?

definitions.text: To be able to, to have the capacity to.
examples: Unfortunately, only one of these gloves will actually fit over my hand.

definitions.text: Expressing a present tense or perfect tense with some conditional or subjective weakening: "will turn out to", "must by inference".
examples: He will be home by now. He always gets home before 6 o'clock.
I can't find my umbrella. I will've left it at home this morning.

definitions.text: One's independent faculty of choice; the ability to be able to exercise one's choice or intention.
examples: Of course, man's will is often regulated by his reason.

definitions.text: The act of choosing to do something; a person’s conscious intent or volition.
examples: Most creatures have a will to live.

definitions.text: One's intention or decision; someone's orders or commands.
examples: Eventually I submitted to my parents' will.

definitions.text: To instruct (that something be done) in one's will.
To bequeath (something) to someone in one's will (legal document).
examples: He willed his stamp collection to the local museum.
